title: Welcome
text:
0.1 About this Manual
This manual is your end-to-end guide for working with the BMS CHO Cell Productivity Knowledge Graph (KG)—a multi-omics, multi-evidence platform built to accelerate the identification of genetic engineering targets that improve CHO cell productivity.
The KG integrates:
The bioinformatics analyses embedded in the KG include differential expression / abundance (DEG/DAA), WGCNA co-expression networks, MOFA multi-omics integration, pathway enrichment (GSEA & ORA), and transcription-factor enrichment.

title: Welcome
text:
BMS in-house data — transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics from Study 1 and Study 2 alongside bioprocess readouts (qP, titre, IVCD, lactate, cell density)
CHO-specific public omics data — 13 datasets from 10+ publications comparing high vs. low producers
Causal literature evidence — cause-effect relationships curated from ~500 genetic perturbation publications
External databases — NCBI, UniProt, GO, KEGG, ChEMBL, PubMed, Cellosaurus

text:

title: Welcome
text:
0.3 Manual Structure
This manual is organised into nine chapters covering all major use-cases, followed by a glossary of key terms.

  1
  Orientation — KG structure, data model, how to phrase queries


  2
  Internal Data Exploration — Browse samples, run DEG / WGCNA / MOFA analyses on BMS data


  3
  Public Literature Evidence — Search published gene-productivity associations


  4
  Cross-Evidence Integration — Concordance between BMS data and literature


  5
  Pathway & Functional Enrichment — KEGG, GO, ORA, GSEA


  6
  Transcription Factor Analysis — Master regulators of productivity


  7
  Hypothesis Testing — Interrogate the KG with a prior gene, pathway, or mechanism


  8
  Target Prioritisation — Shortlist overexpression and knockout candidates


  9
  KG Utilities & Navigation — Search, cross-reference, filter, export

title: Welcome
text:
0.4 Key Conventions






Note



Query boxes throughout this manual show verbatim natural-language queries (NLQ) you can type directly into the KG interface. Results may vary slightly depending on your cohort or filter settings.








Tip



Evidence badges tag information by analytical layer:
DEG/DAA WGCNA MOFA Literature GEM (in progress)



For support, contact yogesh.lakhotia@elucidata.io

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.1 Overview
This chapter is your starting point. Before running any query, spend a few minutes here to understand how the KG is structured—you will get significantly better results.

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.2 What is the Knowledge Graph?
A Knowledge Graph (KG) is a structured, semantic representation of data that connects real-world entities—genes, proteins, metabolites, pathways, samples, publications—through typed relationships.
The BMS CHO Productivity KG uses a property graph model (Neo4j) with three primitives:








Primitive
Description
Examples




Nodes (Entities)
Objects or concepts
Gene, Protein, Metabolite, Sample, Pathway, Factor, Cohort, Study


Edges (Connections)
Typed relationships
gene_expressed_in, upregulated, gene_part_of, tf_regulates_cohort_degs


Properties (Attributes)
Metadata on nodes/edges
log2FC, adj. p-value, kME, MOFA weight, Spearman r



The KG is queried through a natural-language interface that converts your question into Cypher graph queries automatically. You can also use the NLQ tips in Section 2.6.

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.3 Data Model Overview
The complete data model connects the following entity types:

Figure 1.1 — Simplified entity-relationship diagram of the BMS KG. Arrows represent named edge types; colors group entity categories (blue = molecular features, pink = experimental design, orange/tan = phenotypes, purple = pathway annotations).

Key relationships to know:

Gene → Sample: gene_expressed_in (TPM values, per sample)
Gene → Cohort: upregulated / downregulated / non_regulated (DESeq2 results)
Gene / Protein / Metabolite → Network: gene_part_of (WGCNA module membership, kME)
Gene / Protein / Metabolite → Factor: gene_contributes_to_factor (MOFA weights)
Gene → Positive/Negative Phenotypes: Spearman correlation with qP, titre, IVCD
Gene → Cohort: tf_regulates_cohort_degs (TF enrichment)
Pathway → Cohort / Network / Factor: kegg_pathway_enriched_in / go_term_enriched_in

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.5 Cohort Naming Conventions
The KG stores results for 883 cohorts across four categories. When querying for DEG or enrichment results, always name your cohort explicitly for best retrieval.








Category
Count
Example




Productivity-Based (High vs. Low, all clones & days)
6
Nivo_study1_all_high_vs_Nivo_study1_all_low


Clone-Wise (across all days)
36
Nivo_clone8_study1_all_vs_Nivo_clone49_study1_all


Temporal (same clone, different days)
190
Nivo_clone49_day11_study1_vs_Nivo_clone49_day8_study1


Temporal Cross-Clone
630
Nivo_clone8_day11_study1_vs_Nivo_clone49_day8_study1


Public (high vs. low / non-producer)
19
GSE295486_high_producer_vs_GSE295486_non_producer









Tip



Prefer Productivity-Based cohorts when asking global “which genes drive high productivity?” questions. Use Clone-Wise or Temporal cohorts for clone-specific or time-resolved investigations.

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.6 How to Phrase Natural-Language Queries
The KG interface accepts plain English. Follow these principles for reliable results:

Name the entity type — say “gene”, “protein”, or “metabolite”, not just a symbol.
Name the cohort or condition — include molecule (Nivo / C144) and study when relevant.
State the direction — “upregulated in high producers”, “negatively correlated with qP”.
State the evidence layer — “in WGCNA module M1”, “with MOFA Factor 1 weight > 0.3”.
Add thresholds explicitly — “adj. p-value < 0.05 and |log2FC| > 2”.


Show me genes upregulated in high vs. low producers in Nivo Study 1 with adj. p-value < 0.05 and absolute log2 fold change greater than 2.


Which proteins are positively correlated with qP (Spearman r > 0.5) in Study 1?


What KEGG pathways are enriched in WGCNA Module M1 for the Nivo high vs. low productivity cohort?

title: 2  Orientation
text:
2.7 Understanding Query Results
Results are returned as tables, network views, or charts depending on the query type:

Tables — for DEG lists, correlation rankings, module membership
Network views — for TF-gene regulatory graphs, WGCNA modules, KG subgraphs
Bar / violin charts — for pathway enrichment scores, correlation distributions

Key columns you will encounter:







Column
Meaning




log2FC
Log₂ fold change (DEG); positive = higher in first group


adj_pvalue
Benjamini-Hochberg corrected p-value


kME
Module eigengene connectivity (WGCNA hub score; closer to 1 = stronger hub)


gs_qp / gs_titre / gs_ivcd
Gene significance scores (WGCNA correlation with trait)


mofa_weight
MOFA feature weight; sign indicates direction, magnitude indicates importance


spearman_r
Spearman correlation of feature abundance with productivity phenotype


NES
Normalised enrichment score (GSEA); positive = enriched in first group

title: 3  Internal Data Exploration
text:
3.1 Overview
This chapter covers everything you can learn from BMS in-house data alone. It is organised into four progressive layers of analysis, each building on the previous:

title: 3  Internal Data Exploration
text:
AData browsing — what samples, clones, and molecules are available
BDifferential Expression & Abundance — DEG/DAA which features change between high and low producers
CCo-expression Networks (WGCNA) — WGCNA which genes/proteins/metabolites move together
DMulti-Omics Integration (MOFA) — MOFA latent factors that explain productivity variation across all omics layers

title: 3  Internal Data Exploration
text:
3.2 A · Data Browsing

Samples, Clones, and Timepoints

What samples are available for the Nivo molecule in Study 1?


Which days have both transcriptomics and proteomics measurements in Study 1?

The KG stores sample-level coverage. Study 1 has multi-omics samples at Days 3, 5, 9, 11, and 14. Study 2 proteomics spans Days 5, 7, 9, and 11.


Productivity Measurements Across Clones

Show qP, titre, and IVCD values across all Nivo clones in Study 1.



Molecule-Level Filtering
Use the molecule filter to restrict results to a single drug candidate:

Nivo — nivolumab (anti-PD-1 mAb)
C144 — C144 antibody


Show me differentially expressed genes for C144 high vs. low producers only.

title: 3  Internal Data Exploration
text:
3.3 B · Differential Expression & Abundance

Method Reference



Omics Layer
Method
Covariate
Normalisation




Transcriptomics (DEG)
DESeq2
Time
TPM


Proteomics (DAA)
T-test
Time
Median central log₂


Metabolomics (DAA)
T-test
Time
Median central log₂



Results are stored per cohort as directed edges from features to cohort nodes with properties log2FC, adj_pvalue, and regulation (upregulated / downregulated / non_regulated).


Finding Upregulated Genes in High Producers

Which genes are upregulated in high vs. low Nivo producers in Study 1 with adj. p-value < 0.05 and |log2FC| > 2?



Cross-Cohort Consistency
A gene is considered a robust signal if it is consistently differentially expressed across ≥ 7/10 high-vs-low cohorts. The shortlisting funnel applied to Study 1 data:



Filter step
Genes remaining




All DEGs across 232 cohorts
11,481


adj. p-value < 0.05 &
log2FC


Directional consistency (RNA & protein both up or both down)
775


Upregulated in high producers
330


Concordant RNA–protein & Spearman r > 0.5 with qP
63




Show genes consistently upregulated in at least 7 out of 10 high vs. low producer cohorts in Study 1.



Comparing Across Cohorts

Compare DEG results between the C144 and Nivo high-vs-low cohorts. Which genes are shared?



Protein and Metabolite Differential Abundance

Which proteins are differentially abundant in high vs. low Nivo producers in Study 1?


Show differentially abundant metabolites in the pellet fraction for high vs. low C144 producers.

title: 3  Internal Data Exploration
text:
3.4 C · Co-expression Networks (WGCNA)

What is WGCNA?
Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis (WGCNA) clusters features that vary together across samples into modules. Each module is summarised by its Module Eigengene (ME)—the first principal component of all features in that module—which is then correlated with bioprocess traits (qP, titre, IVCD).
1Correlate — pairwise correlations across 36 samples × 3 omics layers
2Weight — raise correlations to soft-threshold power β (amplifies strong connections, suppresses noise; β=19 genes, β=6 protein, β=14 metabolites)
3Cluster — build Topological Overlap Matrix (TOM) → hierarchical clustering → 20 gene, 5 protein, 5 metabolite modules
4Summarise — reduce each module to its ME (1 value per sample)
5Correlate to traits — Pearson r of each ME vs. qP, titre, IVCD

Published precedent: Melville et al. (2011, Journal of Biotechnology) applied WGCNA to 295 microarrays from 121 CHO cultures and identified six transcriptional modules, five of which correlated with bioprocess variables including qP. The BMS KG replicates and extends this approach across all three omics layers.



Finding Productivity-Correlated Modules

Which WGCNA gene modules are positively correlated with qP in Study 1?


Show all WGCNA networks (gene, protein, and metabolite) with significant correlation to titre.

Key modules identified:



Module
Omics
ME–qP correlation
Biological theme




M1 (Pro)
Gene
Positive
ER protein processing / UPR


M18 (Anti)
Gene
Negative
Cell cycle / DNA replication





Exploring Module Membership

List all genes in WGCNA module M1 with their kME scores.



Hub Genes
Hub genes have the highest kME (module eigengene connectivity) and are most representative of the module’s biological program.

What are the top 10 hub genes in WGCNA module M1?



Gene Significance Scores
Gene significance (GS) scores quantify the correlation between each gene’s expression and a phenotype. Available as gs_qp, gs_ivcd, and gs_titre.

Show genes in module M1 with gs_qp > 0.5, ranked by descending kME.



WGCNA for Proteins and Metabolites
The same WGCNA workflow was applied to proteomics and metabolomics data, yielding 5 protein modules and 5 metabolite modules.

Which protein WGCNA modules correlate with IVCD in Study 1?


Show metabolite module members with significant correlation to qP, ranked by gs_qp.

title: 3  Internal Data Exploration
text:
3.5 D · Multi-Omics Factor Analysis (MOFA)

What is MOFA?
Multi-Omics Factor Analysis (MOFA) is an unsupervised dimensionality-reduction method that integrates transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics simultaneously to uncover latent factors—hidden biological programs that explain variation across samples.
1Input preparation — Top 5,000 variable features (transcriptomics & proteomics), all 596 metabolite features
2MOFA decomposition — Joint matrix factorisation across 3 omics views; K=10 latent factors (sufficient to capture major sources without over-fragmenting biology)
3Factor–trait correlation — Spearman's ρ of each factor score vs. qP, titre, IVCD across 36 samples (Spearman is robust to outliers and non-linear monotonic relationships)
4Feature weight interpretation — top 80th percentile features per factor per omics layer extracted for pathway enrichment


Significant Factors and Their Biological Meaning








Factor
Correlation pattern
Interpretation




Factor 1
qP +0.848 · IVCD +0.860 · Titre +0.904
Full-spectrum productivity signal — higher cell efficiency, volumetric yield, and cumulative biomass


Factor 2
qP positive · IVCD negative
High-efficiency-per-cell phenotype — not driven by biomass accumulation


Factor 3
Proteomics-only
Post-translational regulatory axis decoupled from transcription


Factor 4
Transcriptomics-only
Transcriptional program not yet reflected in protein or metabolites at sampled time points


Factor 5
Titre & qP strongly negative
Pure metabolic suppressor acting on secretory machinery









Important



Factor 1 is the most actionable factor. Genes / proteins / metabolites with high positive Factor 1 weights are pro-productivity candidates; those with high negative weights are anti-productivity candidates.




Querying MOFA Results

Which genes have the highest positive MOFA Factor 1 weights?


Show proteins with MOFA Factor 1 weight above 0.3.


Which metabolites contribute to Factor 5? What pathways are enriched?


Show per-sample MOFA Factor 1 scores and overlay with qP measurements.



Cross-Omics Consistency: DEG × WGCNA × MOFA
The overlap of all three evidence layers produces the highest-confidence candidates:

Pro-productivity triple overlap: 20 genes (upregulated in ≥7/10 cohorts AND in WGCNA M1 AND positive Factor 1 weight)
Anti-productivity triple overlap: 34 genes


Show me genes that are consistently upregulated in high producers (≥7/10 cohorts), members of a pro-productivity WGCNA module, and have positive MOFA Factor 1 weight.

Enriched pathways in the triple-overlap candidates:



Direction
Pathway
Significance




Pro-productivity
Protein processing in ER
★★★


Pro-productivity
Cell adhesion molecules
★★★


Pro-productivity
Lysosome
★★★


Anti-productivity
Cell cycle
★★★


Anti-productivity
DNA replication
★★★


Anti-productivity
Homologous recombination
★★★

title: 4  Public Literature Evidence
text:
4.1 Overview
The KG integrates cause-effect relationships curated from ~500 publications on genetic perturbation in the context of CHO productivity. This chapter covers how to query that evidence layer.

title: 4  Public Literature Evidence
text:
4.2 How Literature Evidence is Structured
Publications were scouted from ~45,000 CHO-related papers through a two-stage filter:

Filter 1 — grouped into three categories:

(A) Gene/protein/metabolite target manipulation and cell engineering studies ✓ included
(B) Bioprocess optimisation
(C) Other studies

Filter 2 — Inclusion: productivity insights through target manipulation or omics-driven cell-line engineering. Exclusion: not directly relevant to target manipulation impacting productivity.

Result: ~500 publications describing genetic interventions (overexpression, knockdown, knockout) and their effects on productivity.
Each extracted relationship is stored as a directed edge with properties:



Property
Description




cause
Gene / protein / metabolite manipulated


manipulation
OE (overexpression), KD (knockdown), KO (knockout)


effect_entity
Productivity phenotype affected


effect_direction
Positive (+) or negative (−)


cell_line
e.g., CHO-K1 GS KO


target_product
e.g., IgG1


reactor
e.g., Fed-batch


pmid
PubMed ID of source publication


evidence_sentence
Source text from the paper

title: 4  Public Literature Evidence
text:
4.3 Key Effect Entities
The KG tracks effects across four biological dimensions:
Productivity: specific_productivity_qp · product_titer · volumetric_productivity · secretion_rate · product_yield · production_stability
Growth: viable_cell_density_vcd · cell_viability · IVCD · specific_growth_rate · apoptosis_rate · cell_cycle_phase_distribution · cloning_efficiency
Metabolic: lactate_production/consumption · ammonia_production · glucose_consumption · glutamine_metabolism · redox_balance · metabolic_flux · lipid_metabolism
Quality: glycosylation_profile · fucosylation · sialylation · product_purity · protein_fragmentation · aggregation_level · host_cell_protein_content

title: 4  Public Literature Evidence
text:
4.4 Querying Literature Evidence

Finding All Genes Linked to Productivity

Which genes are linked to specific productivity (qP) in published CHO studies?


Show all genes with published evidence for improving product titer in CHO cells.



Looking Up a Specific Gene

What does the literature say about HSPA5 (GRP78/BiP) and CHO productivity?


Summarise all published evidence for ST14 in CHO cells, including manipulation type and effect.



Filtering by Phenotype

Which genes, when overexpressed, positively affect product titer in CHO?


Show genes whose knockout reduces protein fragmentation.



Filtering by Source Organism

Show only CHO-specific literature evidence for HSPA5.


Are there human or mouse studies for FOXO1 that might be relevant to CHO productivity?



Retrieving Supporting Evidence Sentences

Show the supporting sentences from publications linking HSPA5 overexpression to increased titre.



Genes with Experimental Manipulation

Which genes have been experimentally overexpressed in CHO cells and shown a positive effect on qP? Show PMID and evidence.

text:
4.5 Worked Example: HSPA5 (GRP78/BiP)
Literature
HSPA5 encodes the ER chaperone GRP78/BiP. Published manipulation studies support overexpression as a productivity lever:



PMID

↑ Titre

24081924

↑ qP
Recombinant protein


1373378
GRP78 OE
↑ Productivity
IgG



Mechanism: UPR activation expands ER folding capacity → improves secretory efficiency.

title: 4  Public Literature Evidence
text:
4.6 Worked Example: ST14
Literature
ST14 encodes Matriptase, a serine protease. Knockout studies show improved product quality:



PMID
Manipulation
Effect




29777593
ST14 KO
↓ Protein fragmentation


29777593
ST14 KO
↑ Product purity



Mechanism: Matriptase degrades secreted product → knockout eliminates fragmentation and improves purity.

title: 5  Cross-Evidence Integration
text:
5.1 Overview
The highest-confidence engineering candidates are those supported by both BMS internal data and independent published literature. This chapter explains how to find, interpret, and resolve agreement—or disagreement—between these two evidence streams.

title: 5  Cross-Evidence Integration
text:
5.2 Why Cross-Evidence Analysis Matters
Internal data alone can surface many differentially expressed genes—but not all are validated targets. Literature alone reflects findings in other cell lines, products, and contexts. When both sources agree in direction, confidence in the target rises substantially.






Note



The KG stores BMS omics results and literature cause-effect relationships as separate edge types on the same gene nodes. This allows a single query to traverse both layers simultaneously.

title: 5  Cross-Evidence Integration
section: 5.3 Checking if a Literature Gene Appears in BMS Data
text:
5.3 Checking if a Literature Gene Appears in BMS Data

Is HSPA5 differentially expressed in BMS Study 1 high vs. low producer cohorts?


Is HSPA5 a member of a WGCNA module correlated with productivity in Study 1?


Does HSPA5 have a significant MOFA Factor 1 weight in Study 1?

title: 5  Cross-Evidence Integration
text:
5.4 Direction Concordance
The most important check: does the direction of the BMS signal agree with the published manipulation effect?








BMS signal
Published effect
Verdict




Gene upregulated in high producers
Overexpression → ↑ qP
✅ Concordant — strong OE candidate


Gene downregulated in high producers
Knockout → ↑ titer
✅ Concordant — strong KO candidate


Gene upregulated in high producers
Overexpression → ↓ qP
⚠️ Discordant — investigate context


Gene not DE in BMS
Overexpression → ↑ qP
🔍 BMS data silent — check other layers




For genes with published overexpression evidence showing positive qP effect, which are also upregulated in high producers in BMS Study 1?

title: 5  Cross-Evidence Integration
text:
5.5 Explaining Discordance
When BMS and literature disagree, common explanations include:

Source organism: human or mouse study; CHO physiology may differ
Product type: a gene relevant for mAb production may behave differently for fusion proteins
Cell line background: CHO-K1 vs. CHO-DG44 vs. CHO-S show regulatory differences
Timepoint: literature perturbation may have been measured at a different culture stage


The literature shows positive productivity effect for gene X, but BMS data show it is downregulated in high producers. Are the literature studies from CHO cells or human cells?

title: 5  Cross-Evidence Integration
section: 5.6 Finding Genes with Multi-Omics + Literature Support
text:
5.6 Finding Genes with Multi-Omics + Literature Support
The most actionable candidates:

Show genes that are (1) consistently upregulated in high producers in BMS Study 1 across ≥7 cohorts, (2) members of a pro-productivity WGCNA module, (3) have positive MOFA Factor 1 weight, AND (4) have published evidence linking them to increased qP or titer.

The intersection of these criteria converges to a small, high-confidence set. For example, the HSPA5 / ST14 pair shown in Section 4.1 each pass all four filters.

title: 5  Cross-Evidence Integration
text:
5.7 Genes Unique to BMS Data
Some BMS signals have no published precedent—these are potentially novel targets.

Show genes consistently upregulated in high producers in BMS Study 1 that are NOT mentioned in any ingested publication.

These novel signals may be CHO-cell-line-specific or molecule-specific effects not yet studied in the field.

title: 5  Cross-Evidence Integration
section: 5.8 Mapping BMS Data to Public Omics
text:
5.8 Mapping BMS Data to Public Omics
The KG includes 13 public omics datasets from external CHO studies. You can check whether a BMS-identified gene is also differentially expressed in independent public datasets.

Is HSPA5 differentially expressed in any of the public high-vs-low producer omics datasets?


Which genes from the BMS Study 1 triple-overlap shortlist are also DE in at least 3 public CHO datasets?

title: 6  Pathway & Functional Enrichment
text:
6.1 Overview
Pathway enrichment results are pre-computed and stored directly in the KG as edges between pathway nodes and cohort, network, or factor nodes. You do not need to run enrichment yourself—you query the results.

title: 6  Pathway & Functional Enrichment
text:
6.2 Enrichment Methods









Method
Full name
Input
What it finds




GSEA
Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
Ranked gene list (by log2FC or MOFA weight)
Pathways with coordinated shifts across the full ranked list


ORA
Over-Representation Analysis
Binary gene list (DEGs or module members)
Pathways with more hits than expected by chance



Both methods are applied to:

DEG cohorts (per molecule, per condition)
WGCNA modules (significant modules only)
MOFA factors (top 80th percentile features per factor per omics layer)

Databases used: KEGG Pathways, Gene Ontology Biological Process (GO:BP), Gene Ontology Molecular Function (GO:MF), Gene Ontology Cellular Component (GO:CC).

title: 6  Pathway & Functional Enrichment
text:
6.3 Querying Pathway Enrichment

On DEG Cohorts

Which KEGG pathways are enriched in high vs. low Nivo producers in Study 1?


Show GO Biological Process terms enriched in the downregulated genes of C144 high vs. low producers.


Filter enrichment results for the Nivo high-vs-low cohort to GSEA results only.



On WGCNA Modules

What KEGG pathways are enriched in WGCNA gene module M1?


Show GO terms enriched in the anti-productivity WGCNA gene module M18.



On MOFA Factors

Which pathways are enriched in the top-weighted genes for MOFA Factor 1?


Show pathway enrichment results for MOFA Factor 5, separated by positive and negative weight genes.

title: 6  Pathway & Functional Enrichment
text:
6.4 Key Biological Themes
Results from DEG, WGCNA, and MOFA all converge on consistent pathway themes:

Pro-Productivity Pathways








Pathway
Source
NES / Significance




Protein processing in endoplasmic reticulum
MOFA F1 GSEA
+3.3 ★★★


Cell adhesion molecules
MOFA F1 GSEA
+3.1 ★★★


Lysosome
MOFA F1 GSEA
+3.0 ★★★


Protein export
WGCNA M1 ORA
Significant


Antigen processing and presentation
WGCNA M1 ORA
Significant





Anti-Productivity Pathways



Pathway
Source
NES / Significance




Cell cycle
MOFA F1 GSEA
−3.2 ★★★


DNA replication
MOFA F1 GSEA
−3.0 ★★★


Homologous recombination
MOFA F1 GSEA
−2.9 ★★★


Fanconi anemia pathway
MOFA F1 GSEA
−2.7 ★★★


Mismatch repair
MOFA F1 GSEA
−2.6 ★★★









Tip



Biological interpretation: High producers allocate resources to ER protein folding and secretion (UPR / secretory pathway upregulated) while suppressing cell proliferation programs (cell cycle / DNA replication downregulated). This is consistent with the canonical productivity vs. growth trade-off in CHO cells.

title: 6  Pathway & Functional Enrichment
text:
6.5 Comparing Enriched Pathways Across Molecules

Compare enriched KEGG pathways between Nivo and C144 high-vs-low producer cohorts. Which pathways are shared, and which are molecule-specific?

title: 6  Pathway & Functional Enrichment
text:
6.6 KEGG Metabolic Network Traversal
Beyond pathway enrichment, the KG stores the full KEGG metabolic network (enzymes, reactions, substrates, products), enabling mechanistic queries.

Which KEGG pathway does gene LDHA belong to?


What enzymatic activity does LDHA have, and which metabolic reactions does it catalyse?


Find all genes in the glycolysis / gluconeogenesis KEGG pathway that are differentially expressed in high vs. low Nivo producers.


Trace LDHA to its substrate and product: what metabolites are connected?


Identify metabolic bottlenecks by combining DEG and KEGG data for the lactate metabolism pathway.

title: 7  Transcription Factor Analysis
text:
7.1 Overview
Transcription factor (TF) enrichment analysis identifies upstream regulators whose target genes are over-represented in a DEG list or WGCNA module. This chapter covers how to find, filter, and interpret TF regulatory evidence in the KG.

title: 7  Transcription Factor Analysis
section: 7.2 What TF Enrichment Data is Available
text:
7.2 What TF Enrichment Data is Available
TF enrichment is performed using gseapy (enrich) against curated TF–target databases. Results are stored as edges from TF gene nodes to cohort or network nodes:







Edge type
Meaning




tf_regulates_cohort_degs
TF whose targets are enriched in a specific cohort’s DEG list


tf_regulates_network_genes
TF whose targets are enriched in a WGCNA module



Key properties on TF edges: padj (adjusted p-value), overlap_genes (list of shared target genes), n_targets (total targets regulated).
Available for: Study 1 cohorts and WGCNA modules (gene layer only).

title: 7  Transcription Factor Analysis
text:
7.3 Querying TF Enrichment

TFs Regulating High-Producer DEGs

Which transcription factors regulate genes upregulated in high vs. low Nivo producers in Study 1?


Show TFs with adj. p-value < 0.01 whose targets are enriched among upregulated DEGs in C144 high producers.



TFs Linked to a Specific Module

Which TFs regulate genes in WGCNA module M1 (pro-productivity)?


What transcription factors control the anti-productivity gene module M18?



Tracing a Gene to Its Upstream Regulators

What transcription factors regulate HSPA5 in the context of high-producer cohorts?


Trace gene KCTD14 upstream to its transcription factors.

title: 7  Transcription Factor Analysis
text:
7.4 Master Regulator Identification
A master regulator is a TF that controls a large number of target genes across multiple modules or cohorts.

Which transcription factors have the broadest target gene scope across all pro-productivity cohorts in Study 1?

Key master regulators identified across pro-productivity (M1/M7/M8) and anti-productivity (M16/M18) modules:



TF
Module association
padj (representative)




E2F4
Anti-productivity (M18)
3.7e−122

Anti-productivity
Significant


E2F1
Anti-productivity
Significant

Anti-productivity
Significant


NFE2L2 (NRF2)
Pro-productivity (M1)
Significant

Pro-productivity (M1)
Significant

Pro-productivity (M1)
Significant

Pro-productivity (M1)
Significant

Pro-productivity
Significant


KLF5
Pro-productivity
Significant









Note



E2F4 is the most statistically significant TF in the dataset (padj = 3.7×10⁻¹²²), regulating genes in the anti-productivity cell cycle/DNA replication module M18. Targeting E2F4 or its upstream pathway could suppress cell proliferation programs and redirect resources toward productivity.

title: 7  Transcription Factor Analysis
section: 7.5 Checking if a TF is Itself Differentially Expressed
text:
7.5 Checking if a TF is Itself Differentially Expressed

Is NFE2L2 (NRF2) differentially expressed in high vs. low producers in Study 1?


Is FOXM1 a member of any WGCNA module? What is its gs_qp score?


Does E2F4 have a significant MOFA Factor 1 weight?

title: 7  Transcription Factor Analysis
section: 7.6 Combining TF Evidence with Other Layers
text:
7.6 Combining TF Evidence with Other Layers
For the highest-confidence regulatory targets:

Show TFs that (1) regulate upregulated DEGs in high producers, (2) are themselves upregulated in high producers, AND (3) are members of a pro-productivity WGCNA module.

title: 8  Hypothesis Testing
text:
8.1 Overview
This chapter is for users who arrive at the KG with a prior—a gene of interest from a paper, a pathway hypothesis, or a specific biological mechanism they want to test. The KG lets you rapidly check how much evidence exists for any prior across all analytical layers.

title: 8  Hypothesis Testing
section: 8.2 I Have a Gene — What Does the KG Know?
text:
8.2 I Have a Gene — What Does the KG Know?
Use this multi-layer gene audit as your default starting point whenever you have a gene of interest.

Tell me everything the KG knows about gene HSPA5: differential expression, WGCNA module, MOFA weight, phenotype correlations, and published evidence.

The KG will return evidence from:

DEG/DAA Is it DE in high vs. low producers? In how many cohorts? In which direction?
WGCNA Which module does it belong to? What is its kME? Is the module correlated with qP/titre/IVCD?
MOFA Does it have a significant Factor 1 weight? Positive or negative?
Spearman correlation with qP, titre, and IVCD directly.
Literature Has it been manipulated in CHO cells? With what effect?

title: 8  Hypothesis Testing
section: 8.3 I Have a Pathway — Which Genes in it Are Productivity-Associated?
text:
8.3 I Have a Pathway — Which Genes in it Are Productivity-Associated?

Which genes in the “Protein processing in endoplasmic reticulum” KEGG pathway are differentially expressed in BMS Study 1 high vs. low producer cohorts?


Show all genes in the glycolysis / gluconeogenesis pathway with positive Spearman correlation to qP in Study 1.

title: 8  Hypothesis Testing
section: 8.4 I Have a Gene List — Check Against All Evidence Layers
text:
8.4 I Have a Gene List — Check Against All Evidence Layers

I have a list of genes: [HSPA5, DNAJB9, MERTK, FICD, ZCCHC24]. For each, show: (1) DEG status in high vs. low Nivo producers, (2) WGCNA module, (3) MOFA Factor 1 weight, (4) literature evidence.

title: 8  Hypothesis Testing
section: 8.5 I Have a Phenotype — What Genes Are Most Correlated?
text:
8.5 I Have a Phenotype — What Genes Are Most Correlated?

Which genes have the strongest positive Spearman correlation with specific productivity (qP) in Study 1?


Rank proteins by their Spearman correlation with titre in Study 1, descending.


Which metabolites are most negatively correlated with qP?

title: 8  Hypothesis Testing
text:
8.6 Testing Specific Biological Hypotheses

ER Stress / Unfolded Protein Response (UPR) Hypothesis
Prior: High producers activate UPR to expand ER folding capacity.

Show all genes involved in the UPR or ER stress response that are upregulated in high producers in Study 1.


Is the “protein processing in endoplasmic reticulum” KEGG pathway enriched in the pro-productivity WGCNA module M1?


Which UPR-related transcription factors (ATF4, ATF6, XBP1) are differentially expressed in high producers?

KG verdict: ✅ Strongly supported. Module M1 is enriched for ER processing / UPR. HSPA5, DNAJB9, and FICD carry positive MOFA Factor 1 weights. Literature evidence (PMID 32624757, 24081924) corroborates GRP78/BiP overexpression as a productivity lever.



Secretory Pathway Hypothesis
Prior: High producers have enhanced protein secretion machinery.

Are genes in the “protein export” and “antigen processing” pathways upregulated in high producers in Study 1?


Show the top co-expressed genes with HSPA5 in WGCNA module M1.

KG verdict: ✅ Supported. Protein export and antigen processing are both enriched in WGCNA M1 (pro-productivity module).



Metabolic Bottleneck Hypothesis (Lactate / Ammonia / ROS)
Prior: Runaway lactate accumulation limits productivity in low producers.

Which metabolites significantly differ between high and low producers in Study 1? Highlight lactate.


Show genes in the lactate metabolism pathway (KEGG) with negative Spearman correlation to titre.


Are there published interventions (gene KO/OE) that reduce lactate accumulation in CHO cells?




Chaperone / Difficult-to-Express (DTE) Hypothesis
Prior: DTE proteins benefit from co-expression of specific chaperones.

Show all chaperone genes (HSP family, DNAJ family) upregulated in high producers in Study 1.


What publications link chaperone overexpression to improved productivity of difficult-to-express proteins?




Apoptosis-Resistance Hypothesis
Prior: Blocking apoptosis extends culture longevity and increases total titre.

Are anti-apoptotic genes (BCL2, BCL-XL family) upregulated in high producers in any BMS cohort?


Which published studies link apoptosis-resistance interventions (combined knockouts) to reduced lactate and improved productivity?

title: 9  Target Prioritisation
text:
9.1 Overview
This chapter is the deliverable section—where multi-layer evidence converges into a ranked shortlist of overexpression (OE) and knockout (KO) candidates for experimental validation.

title: 9  Target Prioritisation
text:
9.2 What Makes a High-Confidence Candidate?
The KG scores candidates across six dimensions:
1Direction and Strength of Association — Spearman correlation of gene/protein/metabolite abundance with qP, titre, IVCD
2Multi-omics Signal — Consistent DE signal at both RNA and protein level (directional concordance)
3Consensus Signal — Agreement across DEG, WGCNA module membership, and MOFA Factor loading
4Pathway / Functional Role — Target involved in productivity-relevant biology (secretory pathway, ER stress, metabolic flux)
5Impact on Growth and Titer — In-silico GEM perturbation predicting effects on secretion with minimal growth penalty (in progress)
6Causal Mining Evidence — Publication-evidence score from curated genetic manipulation studies

title: 9  Target Prioritisation
text:
9.3 Overexpression Candidates
Overexpression candidates are genes that, when expressed at higher levels, are expected to increase productivity.
Criteria:

Upregulated in high producers in ≥ 7/10 cohorts
Positive Spearman correlation with qP (r > 0.5)
Positive MOFA Factor 1 weight
Member of pro-productivity WGCNA module (e.g., M1)
Published OE evidence showing positive productivity effect (where available)


Identify overexpression candidates: genes upregulated in high producers across ≥7/10 cohorts, with positive MOFA Factor 1 weight and positive qP correlation > 0.5.


Rank overexpression candidates by number of supporting high-producer cohorts, then by abs(log2FC).


Example: HSPA5
DEG WGCNA MOFA Literature







Evidence layer
Value




DEG consistency
Up in 7/10 cohorts


Spearman qP
r = +0.52


Spearman titre
r = +0.38


MOFA Factor 1 weight
+0.392


WGCNA module
M1 (kME = 0.660); ER processing / UPR enriched


TF regulators
NFE2L2 (NRF2), FOXO1, PPARA, FOXA1


Literature
PMIDs 32624757, 24081924, 1373378 — GRP78/BiP OE → ↑ titre, ↑ qP


Recommendation
▲ OVEREXPRESS

title: 9  Target Prioritisation
text:
9.4 Knockout Candidates
Knockout candidates are genes that, when eliminated, are expected to relieve a productivity bottleneck.
Criteria:

Downregulated in high producers in ≥ 7/10 cohorts (or upregulated, reflecting a gene that suppresses productivity)
Negative Spearman correlation with qP (r < −0.5)
Negative MOFA Factor 1 weight
Member of anti-productivity WGCNA module (e.g., M18)
Published KO evidence showing positive productivity effect (where available)


Identify knockout candidates: genes with negative MOFA Factor 1 weight, downregulated in ≥7/10 high-producer cohorts, and negative Spearman qP correlation.


Example: ST14
DEG WGCNA MOFA Literature







Evidence layer
Value




DEG consistency
Down in 10/10 cohorts


Spearman qP
r = −0.51


Spearman titre
r = −0.51


MOFA Factor 1 weight
−0.303 (anti-productivity loading)


WGCNA module
M18 (kME = 0.595); Cell cycle / DNA replication enriched


TF regulators
E2F4 (padj = 3.7×10⁻¹²²), FOXM1, E2F1, MYC


Literature
PMID 29777593 — ST14 KO → ↓ fragmentation, ↑ product purity


Recommendation
▼ KNOCKOUT

title: 9  Target Prioritisation
text:
9.5 Multi-Omics Concordance Filter
Apply concordance filtering to further refine candidates:

From the 63-gene shortlist, which have concordant RNA–protein regulation (mRNA up and protein up for OE candidates, or mRNA down and protein down for KO candidates)?

title: 9  Target Prioritisation
text:
9.6 Molecule-Specific vs. Shared Targets

Compare overexpression candidates between Nivo and C144. Which targets are shared across both molecules, and which are molecule-specific?


Show knockout candidates that appear in both Nivo and C144 high-vs-low cohorts.

Shared targets are preferred for cell-line-wide engineering; molecule-specific targets may be relevant for product-specific quality or expression challenges.

title: 9  Target Prioritisation
text:
9.7 Exporting the Shortlist

Export the top 20 overexpression candidates as a ranked table with columns: gene symbol, DEG cohort count, Spearman qP, MOFA Factor 1 weight, WGCNA module, literature PMID.

The exported table provides a ready-to-use input for experimental planning and prioritisation discussions.

title: 10  KG Utilities & Navigation
text:
10.1 Overview
This chapter covers the practical mechanics of navigating the KG: searching by identifier, cross-referencing databases, filtering results, and exporting data.

title: 10  KG Utilities & Navigation
text:
10.2 Searching by Identifier
The KG supports search by multiple identifier types:



Entity
Supported identifiers




Gene
Gene symbol (e.g., HSPA5), NCBI Gene ID


Protein
UniProt accession (e.g., P11021)


Metabolite
ChEBI ID, metabolite name


Pathway
KEGG pathway ID (e.g., hsa04141), GO term ID




Find gene HSPA5. Show its NCBI Gene ID, UniProt accession of its encoded protein, and all associated KEGG pathways.


Search for metabolite lactate using its ChEBI ID CHEBI:16651.

title: 10  KG Utilities & Navigation
text:
10.3 Cross-Referencing Identifiers

What is the UniProt accession for the protein encoded by HSPA5?


What CHO gene symbol corresponds to NCBI Gene ID 14828?


What is the KEGG ortholog for human LDHA in CHO cells?

title: 10  KG Utilities & Navigation
text:
10.4 Finding Orthologs
The KG maps CHO genes to human and mouse orthologs via KEGG Orthologs (KO terms).

Find the human ortholog of CHO gene LOC100760815.


Which human genes are orthologs of the top 10 hub genes in WGCNA module M1?

This is particularly useful when interpreting CHO findings in light of human biology or when searching for mechanistic context in human databases.

title: 10  KG Utilities & Navigation
text:

By Study

Show DEG results for HSPA5 restricted to BMS internal studies only (exclude public omics data).


Show only public omics datasets with DEG results for KCTD14.

title: 10  KG Utilities & Navigation
text:
10.7 Exporting Query Results
Results can be exported as:

CSV / TSV — gene lists, ranked tables, enrichment results
JSON — for programmatic downstream processing
Network formats — for import into Cytoscape or other graph tools


Export all WGCNA module M1 members with kME, gs_qp, and gs_titre as a CSV.


Export the top 50 genes by positive MOFA Factor 1 weight across all three omics layers.

title: 10  KG Utilities & Navigation
text:
10.8 Tips for Effective NLQ Queries

Be specific about the entity type — “gene”, “protein”, “metabolite”
Name the cohort explicitly — include molecule and study
State directionality — “upregulated”, “positively correlated”, “with negative MOFA weight”
Include thresholds — exact numbers rather than vague qualifiers (“strong”, “significant”)
Layer your filters progressively — start broad, then add constraints
Reference specific modules or factors by name — “WGCNA module M1”, “MOFA Factor 1”







Tip



If a query returns too many results, add a threshold filter. If it returns nothing, relax one constraint at a time (e.g., lower the cohort consistency requirement from 7/10 to 5/10).

title: 11  Glossary
text: 11.1 Key Terms

title: 11  Glossary
text:
adj. p-value (adjusted p-value)

P-value corrected for multiple testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) method. Values below 0.05 are considered statistically significant in this KG.

C144

One of the two BMS antibody molecules for which in-house multi-omics data is available in Study 1.

CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary)

The cell line used for biopharmaceutical protein production. All BMS in-house data in this KG comes from CHO cells.

Cohort

A pairwise comparison group used for differential expression analysis (e.g., Nivo high vs. low producers, or clone A day 5 vs. clone B day 9). The KG contains 883 cohorts.

DAA (Differential Abundance Analysis)

Statistical analysis identifying proteins or metabolites that differ significantly in abundance between two conditions. Method: T-test with time as covariate.

DEG (Differentially Expressed Gene)

A gene whose mRNA expression differs significantly between two conditions. Method: DESeq2 with time as covariate.

DTE (Difficult-to-Express)

Proteins with complex folding or disulfide-bond requirements that make them hard to produce at high levels in CHO cells.

Factor (MOFA Factor)

A latent variable identified by MOFA that captures coordinated variation across multiple omics layers. Factor 1 is the most strongly correlated with productivity phenotypes (qP +0.848, IVCD +0.860, titre +0.904).

GEM (Genome-scale Metabolic Model)

A constraint-based computational model of all metabolic reactions in a cell. Used for in-silico gene perturbation to predict effects on secretion flux. GEM integration is in progress.

GO (Gene Ontology)

A standardised vocabulary of biological terms. Sub-ontologies: BP (Biological Process), MF (Molecular Function), CC (Cellular Component).

GS (Gene Significance)

In WGCNA, the Pearson correlation between a gene’s expression and a trait. Stored as gs_qp, gs_ivcd, and gs_titre.

GSEA (Gene Set Enrichment Analysis)

Enrichment method that uses a ranked gene list to detect coordinated shifts in pathway activity. Reports Normalised Enrichment Score (NES).

IVCD (Integral Viable Cell Density)

The area under the viable cell density–time curve. Measures cumulative biomass produced during a culture run.

KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes)

A database of biological pathways, reactions, enzymes, and compounds. Used for pathway enrichment and metabolic network traversal in this KG.

kME (Module Eigengene Connectivity)

In WGCNA, the correlation between a gene’s expression and its module eigengene. Higher kME (closer to 1) indicates the gene is a better representative (hub gene) of the module.

KO (Knockout)

Complete elimination of a gene’s function, typically via CRISPR-Cas9. Contrast with KD (knockdown, partial reduction) and OE (overexpression).

ME (Module Eigengene)

The first principal component of all features within a WGCNA module. Represents the overall expression pattern of the module across samples.

MOFA (Multi-Omics Factor Analysis)

An unsupervised dimensionality-reduction framework that integrates multiple omics layers into shared latent factors.

Nivo (Nivolumab)

One of the two BMS antibody molecules (anti-PD-1 mAb) for which in-house data is available in Studies 1 and 2.

NES (Normalised Enrichment Score)

The GSEA enrichment score normalised for gene set size and the number of permutations. Positive NES = enrichment in the first group; negative NES = enrichment in the second group.

NLQ (Natural Language Query)

A plain-English question typed into the KG interface, which is automatically converted to a Cypher graph query.

OE (Overexpression)

Engineering a cell to produce more of a specific protein, typically via stable transgene insertion.

ORA (Over-Representation Analysis)

Enrichment method that tests whether a binary gene list contains more pathway members than expected by chance.

qP (Specific Productivity)

The rate of product secretion per cell per unit time (pg/cell/day). A key productivity phenotype in the KG.

Titre

Total product concentration in the culture supernatant at harvest (mg/L). Reflects both qP and viable cell density over time.

TF (Transcription Factor)

A protein that binds DNA regulatory elements to activate or repress gene expression. TF enrichment identifies master regulators of DEG programs.

TOM (Topological Overlap Matrix)

A measure of network interconnectedness used in WGCNA. Genes with high TOM are densely connected and tend to cluster into the same module.

TPM (Transcripts Per Million)

A normalisation unit for RNA-seq expression data. Accounts for sequencing depth and transcript length.

UPR (Unfolded Protein Response)

A cellular stress response activated when misfolded proteins accumulate in the ER. Associated with pro-productivity gene expression programs in high CHO producers.

WGCNA (Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis)

A network-based method that groups co-expressed genes, proteins, or metabolites into modules and correlates module activity with biological traits.
